access.accessMethod: U-M item handle and Corpus of Middle English collection

access.format: HTML digital text; collection XML bulk access
notes: The exact item route returned a 403 challenge to automated inspection, but the stable handle, live collection browse entry, checked-out XML identifier, and current collection-level access statement independently establish the item.

entities.description: Public Middle English text encoding Furnivall's revised-edition title page and complete poem, including editorial marginal summaries, six footnotes, printed page breaks, manuscript leaf milestones, and italic expansion/supply markup.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00054

rights.basis: The current U-M collection page says the materials are in the public domain in the United States and applies No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: Materials presented in the U-M Corpus of Middle English, including item CME00054
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: The statement is jurisdiction-specific and does not govern Lambeth photographs or the modern METS edition.

bibliographic.citation: Adam of Cobsam, The Wright's Chaste Wife, copied and edited by Frederick J. Furnivall, 2nd ed., revised 1869, EETS Original Series 12 (London: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, MDCCCLXV [1865]).
bibliographic.pages: viii, 26 printed pages in the scanned issue
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 12
bibliographic.title: The Wright's Chaste Wife

entities.description: The immediate print source of CME00054: the revised second edition of EETS Original Series 12, explicitly dated 1869 in brackets while retaining the first edition's 1865 imprint.
entities.label: Furnivall's 1869 revised second edition of The Wright's Chaste Wife
notes: The poem occupies printed pp. 1-19. Preface, P.P.S. 1869, notes, glossary, and the companion poem 'Women' are outside CME00054's encoded selection.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1869; the exact Google scan front matter and U-M collection both identify the historical book/material as public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1869 revised second edition, including its editorial contribution
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: Lambeth Palace Library Archives Catalogue exact manuscript record
Ask Lambeth for MS Film 1224 or locate World Microfilms, Lambeth Palace Library: The Mediaeval Manuscripts, Section I, reel 7
access.format: Microfilm; World Microfilms reel also contains MSS 298 and 328
notes: The catalog proves the reproduction route but does not state whether the film includes all bindings and leaves; coverage remains unknown until inspected or confirmed by Lambeth.

dateStatements.label: Work witness dating
dateStatements.notes: The normalized range represents the METS witness dating, not a firm composition range for the poem.
dateStatements.value: 1460s in the modern METS bibliography; Scribe H conservatively dated to the fifteenth century by Lambeth

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of The Wright's Chaste Wife. The poem occupies Booklet 10, ff. 178r-187r, copied by Scribe H; the current codex is a composite of ten or eleven independent booklets bound together c. 1504-1512.

holding.notes: Item 26; Booklet 10; later owned and annotated elsewhere in the codex by John Stow.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library MS 306

physicalDescription.extent: ff. i + 204; The Wright's Chaste Wife on ff. 178r-187r
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex of ten or eleven independent booklets
notes: Blind-stamped leather over wooden boards, rebacked; both clasps missing.
Scribe H writes a fifteenth-century Secretary hand with Anglicana features, approximately 37 lines per page.
The poem begins below a medical recipe on f. 178r and ends above further medical recipes on f. 187r; these shared physical surfaces remain in the full facsimile mapping.

rights.basis: The underlying Middle English work and unique manuscript copy predate modern copyright by centuries; U-M also labels its public-domain edition collection No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: The fifteenth-century textual content of The Wright's Chaste Wife on ff. 178r-187r
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This does not determine rights or contractual conditions in modern photographs, editorial contributions, encodings, or supplied reproductions.

access.accessMethod: Lambeth LUNA compound-book viewer and IIIF Presentation manifest
access.entityLabel: Lambeth complete public digital sequence for MS 306
access.format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and 415 color JPEG images

localCopies.label: Lambeth Palace Library MS 306 complete public IIIF sequence
notes: ZIP integrity passed; inventory, source, canvas, and image counts are all 415.

The provider labels canvases only with generic numbers 1-415. Visual and sequence audit maps canvas 1 to front cover, 2 to inside front board/pastedown, 3-4 to fol. i recto/verso, 5 to fol. 1r, 411-412 to fol. 204r/v, 413 to inside rear board/pastedown and fragment, 414 to rear cover, and 415 to spine.

Requested IIIF profile was width-only '1800,' for 414 canvases. The narrow spine did not expose that derivative and was retained at native 'full' size; the inventory records every exact successful request URL and effective profile.

Sampled ordinary images decode at 1800 x 2700 pixels; the rear cover is 1800 x 2699 and the native spine is 1249 x 5559. The files are practical color research derivatives, not asserted preservation masters.
Coverage is complete for the provider-published physical manuscript sequence. No separate top, fore, or bottom edge photographs are exposed.

workPortion.label: The Wright's Chaste Wife within the complete MS 306 facsimile
notes: Canvas 359 / f. 178r contains a preceding medical recipe above the poem opening; canvas 377 / f. 187r contains the poem ending and following medical material on the same leaf.
The complete bundle retains all adjacent works, blanks, binding matter, and physical surfaces exposed by the provider.
Public viewing and technical download do not establish permission to redistribute Lambeth photographs. The ignored local bundle is for private research pending permission.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF canvases identify rights only as 'LPL' and supply no open license. Lambeth's official forms restrict supplied copies to private study/research and require separate, use-specific permission for publication or other reproduction.
rights.component: All 415 Lambeth LUNA manuscript photographs, IIIF manifest metadata, and interface-associated reproductions
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: Public viewing and private research caching do not authorize committing the bundle, redistributing it through the corpus repository, or using the images in profit-oriented print-on-demand products.
Lambeth's permission is non-exclusive, use-specific, non-transferable where a fee is paid, and must be renewed for revised editions or additional formats.

entities.description: The complete 415-canvas color sequence exposed by Lambeth, including the front cover, inside front board and pastedown, preliminary leaf, ff. 1r-204v, inside rear board and pastedown with fragment, rear cover, and spine. The poem occupies canvases 359-377 / provider page indices 358-376.
entities.label: Lambeth complete public digital sequence for MS 306

rights.basis: The exact IIIF canvases identify rights only as 'LPL' and supply no open license. Lambeth's official forms restrict supplied copies to private study/research and require separate, use-specific permission for publication or other reproduction.
rights.component: All 415 Lambeth LUNA manuscript photographs, IIIF manifest metadata, and interface-associated reproductions
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse

entities.description: Keller's Latin edition containing chapter 69, De castitate, printed pp. 107-109. Furnivall's 1869 postscript identifies this chapter as the immediate original of The Wright's Chaste Wife; that source claim is not treated as settled.

access.accessMethod: Complete and return the digital-photography form; submit the separate permission form for publication or repository reproduction
access.entityLabel: Lambeth Palace Library reprographic and publication-permission service
access.format: High-quality TIFF/JPEG or standard PDF by request; separate use-specific permission

localCopies.label: Lambeth Palace Library digital photography order form
notes: Complete official form and conditions, retained as a supporting access source rather than manuscript content.

localCopies.label: Lambeth Palace Library permission to reproduce form
notes: Complete official form and use conditions, retained as a supporting rights source rather than manuscript content.

The form lists high-quality TIFF/JPEG at £14.40 each including VAT (£12 excluding VAT) and standard research PDFs at £8.40 for the first item and £1.20 for subsequent images including VAT; payment is quoted before work begins.
The published form limits ordinary orders to 40 scans and allows refusal, so a whole 415-image preservation-resolution order or missing edge views require advance staff discussion.
The standard PDF is stated to be for basic research and unsuitable for reproduction. High-quality images and publication permission are separate requests.

entities.description: The official order and permission route for high-quality manuscript images, research PDFs, and permission to reproduce Lambeth images. It is an access service, not a textual ancestor.
entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library reprographic and publication-permission service

next_steps: Compare the English poem against multiple medieval witnesses of Gesta chapter 69 rather than only Keller's edited text.
Consult current source scholarship before adding a transcribes, version_of, or other direct derivation edge.
notes: The manifest records Furnivall's citation exactly while withholding a stronger derivation claim.
openQuestions.question: Is Keller's Gesta Romanorum chapter 69 a direct source of The Wright's Chaste Wife or one analogue within a wider tale tradition?

next_steps: Locate a verified scan of the 1865 first edition and compare it page by page with the cached 1869 revised second edition.
Consult EETS archives or correspondence for the revision method.
openQuestions.question: Which readings and apparatus changed between Furnivall's 1865 first edition and the 1869 revised second edition, and did Furnivall recheck MS 306?

next_steps: Submit the permission form naming the exact repository URL, complete 415-image private cache, proposed selection, print-on-demand formats, profit possibilities, and anticipated print runs.
Until written permission is received, keep all image bytes Git-ignored and publish only catalog, IIIF, folio, canvas, and request links.
openQuestions.question: Will Lambeth Palace Library permit redistribution of the complete or selected MS 306 images in the public General Corpus repository or their use in commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publications?

next_steps: Ask Lambeth whether preservation-master files already exist for all 415 published images and whether separate edge photographs can safely be made.
Request covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, every folio side, adjacent matter, and available edges as one physical-object scope; give ff. 178r-187r only as the work mapping.
Discuss the order-form 40-scan limit before requesting a whole-codex replacement delivery.
notes: The current public bundle is complete for the 415-canvas provider sequence and already includes covers, pastedowns, all leaves, rear matter, and a separate spine image.
openQuestions.question: Can Lambeth provide a higher-resolution whole-manuscript delivery and missing top, fore, and bottom edge views while preserving the full physical-object sequence?

reviewNotes: Lambeth's IIIF canvas labels are generic numbers 1-415. The folio mapping was established from the full sequence and verified visually against front and rear physical matter, poem boundaries, the catalog locus, and the regular foliation sequence.

The cached MS 306 bundle includes every provider canvas: front cover, inside front board and pastedown, preliminary leaf, all folios, inside rear board and pastedown with fragment, rear cover, and spine. No separate top, fore, or bottom edge views are exposed.
Furnivall's title page says '[Second Edition, revised, 1869]' while retaining an MDCCCLXV imprint. DIMEV independently distinguishes the 1865 first edition from the 1869 revised second edition.
The U-M browse label '(1875)' conflicts with the inspected title page and DIMEV and is recorded as a catalog-layer error, not an edition date.

localCopies.label: Lambeth Palace Library MS 306 complete public IIIF sequence
notes: ZIP integrity passed; inventory, source, canvas, and image counts are all 415.

The provider labels canvases only with generic numbers 1-415. Visual and sequence audit maps canvas 1 to front cover, 2 to inside front board/pastedown, 3-4 to fol. i recto/verso, 5 to fol. 1r, 411-412 to fol. 204r/v, 413 to inside rear board/pastedown and fragment, 414 to rear cover, and 415 to spine.

Requested IIIF profile was width-only '1800,' for 414 canvases. The narrow spine did not expose that derivative and was retained at native 'full' size; the inventory records every exact successful request URL and effective profile.

Sampled ordinary images decode at 1800 x 2700 pixels; the rear cover is 1800 x 2699 and the native spine is 1249 x 5559. The files are practical color research derivatives, not asserted preservation masters.
Coverage is complete for the provider-published physical manuscript sequence. No separate top, fore, or bottom edge photographs are exposed.

workPortion.label: The Wright's Chaste Wife within the complete MS 306 facsimile
notes: Canvas 359 / f. 178r contains a preceding medical recipe above the poem opening; canvas 377 / f. 187r contains the poem ending and following medical material on the same leaf.
The complete bundle retains all adjacent works, blanks, binding matter, and physical surfaces exposed by the provider.
Public viewing and technical download do not establish permission to redistribute Lambeth photographs. The ignored local bundle is for private research pending permission.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF canvases identify rights only as 'LPL' and supply no open license. Lambeth's official forms restrict supplied copies to private study/research and require separate, use-specific permission for publication or other reproduction.

rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: Public viewing and private research caching do not authorize committing the bundle, redistributing it through the corpus repository, or using the images in profit-oriented print-on-demand products.
Lambeth's permission is non-exclusive, use-specific, non-transferable where a fee is paid, and must be renewed for revised editions or additional formats.

localCopies.label: Lambeth Palace Library permission to reproduce form
notes: Complete official form and use conditions, retained as a supporting rights source rather than manuscript content.

The form lists high-quality TIFF/JPEG at £14.40 each including VAT (£12 excluding VAT) and standard research PDFs at £8.40 for the first item and £1.20 for subsequent images including VAT; payment is quoted before work begins.
The published form limits ordinary orders to 40 scans and allows refusal, so a whole 415-image preservation-resolution order or missing edge views require advance staff discussion.
The standard PDF is stated to be for basic research and unsuitable for reproduction. High-quality images and publication permission are separate requests.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00054. The U-M encoding reproduces the title page and complete 672-line poem from Frederick J. Furnivall's 1869 revised second edition of EETS Original Series 12, while omitting the edition's preface, notes, glossary, companion poem, and other matter. That revised edition descends from Furnivall's 1865 first edition, which he transcribed directly from the unique witness, Lambeth Palace Library MS 306, fols. 178r-187r. A complete 415-image color IIIF bundle of MS 306 is cached privately at a practical access resolution; the work is mapped inside the full codex at canvases 359-377 rather than cropped out. DIMEV v1.0 and Eve Salisbury's 2002 METS edition are collateral modern sources. Furnivall's claim that Keller's Gesta Romanorum chapter 69 was the poem's immediate original remains explicitly unresolved.

catalogSummary.author: Adam of Cobsam (attributed; authorship uncertain)
catalogSummary.date: Traditionally dated c. 1462; uniquely attested in a manuscript copy dated to the 1460s
catalogSummary.region: England; precise place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Wright's Chaste Wife

catalogingSubject.label: The Wright's Chaste Wife
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The late Middle English comic verse tale conventionally called The Wright's Chaste Wife. The record describes the intellectual work represented by CME00054, not Lambeth Palace Library MS 306 as a whole, Furnivall's revised second edition, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, or the medical recipes and other works adjacent to the poem in the codex.

dateStatements.display: Traditionally c. 1462, inferred from the poem's concluding White Rose and Edward IV allusion; exact composition date uncertain
notes: Furnivall's title page prints 'about 1462 A.D.' and his preface dates the handwriting soon after 1460. Neither statement securely proves the work's composition year.

dateStatements.display: 1460s, the date assigned by the modern METS bibliography to the unique Lambeth MS 306 witness
notes: This dates the surviving copy and first known attestation, not necessarily composition. The Lambeth catalog conservatively dates Scribe H to the fifteenth century.

dateStatements.display: First published by Frederick J. Furnivall in EETS Original Series 12 (1865)

extent.description: One poem. The repository XML contains 672 L elements in six large LG containers; DIMEV independently describes 672 lines in six-line aabccb stanzas. The six XML LG containers are encoding groupings, not a claim that the poem has only six stanzas.

formStatements.description: A 672-line narrative poem in six-line aabccb stanzas; editorial prose summaries and notes in Furnivall and CME are not part of the work's form.

genres.label: Social satire
notes: METS places the poem in 'Satire and Fabliaux in Verse and Prose'; its notes cite DuVal's description of it as a satiric fabliau.
The manuscript's exact work locus is ff. 178r-187r within Booklet 10; the complete public facsimile is mapped in the linked lineage manifest rather than reduced to a work-only excerpt.
Furnivall's printed italic letters frequently represent manuscript abbreviation expansions or supplied letters, not modern semantic emphasis.

The U-M browse page currently labels the Furnivall item '(1875)'; the inspected title page and DIMEV establish first edition 1865 and revised second edition 1869, so 1875 is retained only as a catalog-layer error in lineage evidence.

next_steps: Consult specialist authorship studies and any paleographical analysis of the name in Lambeth MS 306.
Retain attributed authorship unless direct evidence distinguishes the role.
openQuestions.question: Does the line naming Adam of Cobsam identify the author, a performer or teller, or an earlier source authority?

next_steps: Review current scholarship on the political allusion, language, and Scribe H's booklet context.
Do not infer London composition from the miscellany's London materials or later ownership.
openQuestions.question: Can composition be dated or localized more securely than the traditional c. 1462/1460s inference from the unique witness and White Rose allusion?

next_steps: Compare the English poem with multiple Gesta chapter-69 witnesses and other chastity-test and trapped-suitor analogues.
Seek modern source scholarship before strengthening the relation beyond Furnivall's 1869 assertion.
openQuestions.question: Is Keller's Gesta Romanorum chapter 69 a direct textual source for The Wright's Chaste Wife or one analogue within a broader tale tradition?

notes: The poem is Middle English and its conclusion invokes England; this does not establish a precise place of composition. London associations in the codex and modern criticism are not promoted to a composition claim.

assertion.notes: The line 'Thys seyd Adam of Cobsam' supports traditional attribution but does not unambiguously distinguish author, teller, or source authority; METS categorizes the authorship as uncertain.

scope.description: The 1865 first edition and the immediate 1869 revised second edition

notes: Furnivall states that he copied the poem from the manuscript in one afternoon after William Stubbs showed him the volume.

scope.description: Direct transcription of Lambeth MS 306, fols. 178r-187r

subjects.label: Domestic labor, craft, and gender roles
notes: The wife forces the lord, steward, and proctor to process flax and spin for food; METS emphasizes female domestic labor in its contextual framing.

subjects.label: Social rank, labor, and comic humiliation
White Rose and Yorkist political allusion
notes: The political interpretation is Furnivall's; the poem itself names white roses, England, God, and the commons without explicitly naming Edward IV.

summaries.value: A wright marries a reputedly faithful woman and receives a rose garland whose color is said to reveal her fidelity. While he is away building for his lord, the lord, steward, and proctor each offer his wife money for sex. She accepts their gifts, traps each would-be lover in a deep chamber, and makes the three men process flax and spin in exchange for food. When the wright returns, the men are exposed and released, the wife keeps their money, and the unfaded garland confirms her fidelity.

CME00054 contains Furnivall's edition title page and the complete 672-line poem with marginal summaries and six footnotes. It does not encode Furnivall's preface, printed notes, glossary, the companion poem 'Women,' or the surrounding contents of Lambeth MS 306.

notes: This is a later manuscript title, not evidence for the wording of an authorial title.

scope.description: Title added by a later hand at Lambeth MS 306, fol. 178r

titles.value: A ffable of a Wryght that was maryde to a pore wydows dowter the whiche wydow havyng noo good to geve with her gave as for a precyous Iohell to hym a Rose garlond the whyche sche affermyd wold never fade while sche kept truly her wedlok

work.summary: A wright marries a reputedly faithful woman and receives a rose garland whose color is said to reveal her fidelity. While he is away building for his lord, the lord, steward, and proctor each offer his wife money for sex. She accepts their gifts, traps each would-be lover in a deep chamber, and makes the three men process flax and spin in exchange for food. When the wright returns, the men are exposed and released, the wife keeps their money, and the unfaded garland confirms her fidelity.
